---
title: "Build a site like this one"
subtitle: "A step-by-step walkthrough: Quarto + GitHub Pages"
toc: true
---

This walks through everything needed to go from nothing to a live Quarto website on GitHub Pages, the same way this site (`kokoromi`) is set up. Commands are shown for the shell (bash/PowerShell); where R users have a native alternative (e.g. via the `quarto` and `usethis` packages), that's shown in its own tab.

::: {.callout-note}
You need a **GitHub account** and a repo named exactly `<your-username>.github.io` for the "user site" setup used here (serves at the root domain, no project path).
:::

## 1. Install the prerequisites

::: {.panel-tabset}
## macOS
```bash
brew install --cask quarto
brew install git gh          # gh = GitHub CLI, optional but handy
```

## Windows
```powershell
winget install --id Posit.Quarto
winget install --id Git.Git
winget install --id GitHub.cli
```

## Linux
```bash
sudo apt install git
# Quarto: download the .deb from https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
sudo dpkg -i quarto-*.deb
```
:::

Confirm everything is on your `PATH`:

```bash
quarto --version
git --version
```

::: {.callout-tip}
If you plan to write posts that execute **R** code, also install R and, inside R, `install.packages("rmarkdown")`. For **Python** code chunks, you need `jupyter` (`pip install jupyter`). Neither is required for plain Markdown pages or Observable JS demos — see the [Showcase](showcase.qmd) page.
:::

## 2. Create the GitHub repo

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Bash (gh CLI)
```bash
gh repo create <your-username>.github.io --public --clone
cd <your-username>.github.io
```

## R (usethis)
```r
usethis::create_github_repo(
  "<your-username>.github.io",
  private = FALSE
)
```
:::

If you'd rather not use a CLI, create the repo in the GitHub web UI, then clone it:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-username>.github.io.git
cd <your-username>.github.io
```

## 3. Scaffold a Quarto website project

Inside the repo folder:

```bash
quarto create project website .
```

This generates `_quarto.yml`, an `index.qmd`, an `about.qmd`, and a starter `styles.css`. Answer the prompts (project title, etc.) — you can always change them later.

::: {.callout-tip}
## R alternative
From the R console, the [`quarto`](https://quarto-dev.github.io/quarto-r/) package wraps the same CLI:

```r
quarto::quarto_create_project(name = ".", type = "website")
```
:::

## 4. Point the build output at `docs/`

GitHub Pages needs a specific folder to serve from. Rather than fiddling with branches, the simplest option is: render straight into `docs/` and tell GitHub Pages to serve that folder. In `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
```

## 5. Configure navigation

Still in `_quarto.yml`, set up a navbar and/or a docked sidebar — only listing pages that actually exist:

```yaml
website:
  title: "your-site-name"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
  sidebar:
    style: "docked"
    search: true
    contents:
      - index.qmd
      - about.qmd

format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly   # gives you a free light/dark toggle
    css: styles.css
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Every file listed under `navbar` or `sidebar` must exist as an actual `.qmd` in the repo. A stale reference (say, from a template you copy-pasted) won't error loudly — it'll just render as a dead link, and it's easy to miss until someone clicks it.
:::

## 6. Write your pages

Each page is a `.qmd` file with a YAML header and Markdown/code below:

```markdown
---
title: "About"
---

Some content here.
```

Add as many as you like, and list them in `_quarto.yml` so they show up in navigation.

## 7. Set up `.gitignore` correctly

Quarto's local working directory (`_site/` if you render outside the project, `.quarto/` cache) shouldn't be committed — but your **output** (`docs/`) must be:

```text
/.quarto/
_site/
**/*.quarto_ipynb
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## A real pitfall
Don't ever paste `git status` output *into* `.gitignore` — it's an easy copy/paste slip, and the result silently excludes real site files (like `index.html` or newly generated CSS) from every future commit. The symptom is subtle: your site renders fine locally but looks broken/unstyled once pushed, because new asset files never made it into the repo.
:::

## 8. Render and preview locally — before pushing

```bash
quarto render        # full project render (not `quarto render index.qmd`!)
quarto preview        # opens a live-reloading local server
```

::: {.callout-important}
Always render the **whole project** (`quarto render` with no arguments), not a single file. Rendering one `.qmd` directly skips the site-wide navbar/sidebar/theme wiring, and that page will come out looking unstyled compared to the rest of the site.
:::

## 9. Commit and push

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Initial site"
git push origin main
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## R alternative
```r
quarto::quarto_render()
gert::git_add(".")
gert::git_commit("Initial site")
gert::git_push()
```
:::

## 10. Turn on GitHub Pages

In the GitHub web UI:

1. Go to your repo → **Settings** → **Pages**.
2. Under **Build and deployment**, set **Source** to `Deploy from a branch`.
3. Set **Branch** to `main` and the folder to `/docs`.
4. Save, then wait a minute or two.

Your site will be live at `https://<your-username>.github.io/`.

::: {.callout-note}
## Alternative: GitHub Actions
Instead of committing rendered HTML at all, you can let GitHub build it for you on every push using the [`quarto-actions`](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-actions) workflow, and deploy from a `gh-pages` branch instead of `docs/`. That keeps your `main` branch free of generated files — more setup up front, but a cleaner history long-term.
:::

## Checklist

- [ ] Quarto CLI + Git installed
- [ ] Repo named `<username>.github.io`
- [ ] `_quarto.yml` has `output-dir: docs`
- [ ] Navbar/sidebar only reference pages that exist
- [ ] `.gitignore` excludes `_site/` and `.quarto/`, **not** `docs/`
- [ ] `quarto render` run on the full project, no errors/warnings
- [ ] Pushed to `main`
- [ ] GitHub Pages source set to `main` / `docs`

## Where to go next

- [Quarto websites guide](https://quarto.org/docs/websites)
- [Showcase page](showcase.qmd) — callouts, tabsets, live demos
- [GitHub Pages docs](https://docs.github.com/en/pages)
